data.title: Delta Air Lines leads the airline sector after issuing a strong 2025 earnings forecast
data.description:
Delta Air Lines (NYSE:DAL) fell in early trading on Friday after the airline company reported revenue of $15.56 billion to top the consensus estimate by $1.08 billion. Earnings per share came in at $1.85, vs. $1.74 consensus and $1.28 a year ago.

The airline company noted that demand accelerated through the quarter. During the months of November and December, Delta (NYSE:DAL) saw four of the top ten revenue days in the company's history and double-digit growth in cash bookings, driven by both leisure and corporate travelers. Notably, corporate sales were up by a double-digit rate during the quarter.

All three of Delta's (NYSE:DAL) international geographies improved sequentially and relative to initial expectations, with international passenger revenue growth of 6% year-over-year in the December quarter.

Delta (DAL) reported an adjusted operating margin of 12.0% for the quarter, vs. 9.9% a year ago as lower fuel costs factored in. Total revenue per available seat mile was $0.2004 vs. $0.1995 a year ago, while adjusted cost per available seat mile was $0.1372 vs. $0.1329 a year ago.

"As we move into 2025, we expect strong demand for travel to continue, with consumers increasingly seeking the premium products and experiences that Delta provides," highlighted CEO Ed Bastian. "Our differentiated strategy and best-in-class operations, combined with demand strength and an increasingly constructive industry backdrop, position us to deliver the best financial year in Delta's 100-year history, with pre-tax income greater than $6 billion, earnings per share greater than $7.35 and free cash flow of more than $4 billion," he added.

On the guidance front, Delta Air Lines (DAL) sees Q1 EPS of $0.70 to $1.00 (midpoint $0.85) vs. $0.76 consensus and 2025 adjusted EPS above $7.35 vs. $7.45 consensus.

Shares of Delta Air Lines (DAL) rose 3.35% in premarket trading. Across the airline sector, American Airlines Group (AAL), United Airlines (UAL), JetBlue Airways (JBLU), and Southwest Airlines (LUV) also showed gains.

data.title: AccuWeather updates California wildfire damage estimate to $135B-$150B
data.description:
The death toll from three days of blazing wildfires in Los Angeles has climbed to 10 people, while many communities remain under red flag warnings amid concerns over air quality. The most damaging fires in Eaton and Palisades have destroyed over 10,000 structures in scenes that have been described as "apocalyptic" and reduced entire neighborhoods to ash. A tough road lies ahead for swaths of America's most populated county, as well as its tens of thousands of residents who have been displaced by the infernos.

Quote: "The obvious question is, 'Do you think it's going to grow?'" Los Angeles County Sheriff Robert Luna said at a news briefing on Thursday evening. "I am praying it doesn't but based on the devastation that is clear – it looks like a bomb, an atomic bomb - dropped in these areas. I don't expect good news."

Economic estimates continue to climb, with the latest numbers from the AccuWeather forecasting service pegging total damages at $135B-$150B. At those levels, the wildfire disaster could reach nearly 4% of the annual GDP of the state of California. There is no doubt that will further shake up the insurance and reinsurance industry, which has already been raising the cost of coverage for dangerous areas, and dropping or refusing to issue new policies.

What to watch: Significant double-digit increases in homeowners insurance are on tap in the coming years, which is likely to weigh on property values or neighborhoods. New rules in California also seek to require insurers to underwrite a minimum percentage of policies in high-risk areas based on their state market share, but that threatens to raise premiums. Funding for firefighting and emergency resources will need to be shored up as well, including the monitoring of utilities and prevention, putting additional strain on state and federal budgets. According to JPMorgan, the publicly traded insurers most exposed to California include Allstate (NYSE:ALL), Chubb (NYSE:CB) and Travelers (NYSE:TRV).

More on the wildfires

  • LA wildfires double insurance bill to $20B overnight, J.P. Morgan estimates
  • California utility Edison plunges 10% but analysts OK with wildfire liability protections
  • LA wildfires cost to rank among worst US disasters - report

data.title: Nvidia criticizes Biden administration's plan for AI chip export curbs - report
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) criticized a potential plan for new AI chip export curbs, which could be announced soon by the outgoing Joe Biden administration, Bloomberg News reported.

"We would encourage President Biden to not preempt incoming President Trump by enacting a policy that will only harm the U.S. economy, set America back, and play into the hands of US adversaries," said Ned Finkle, Nvidia's vice president of government affairs, according to the report.

Nvidia and the U.S. Department of Commerce have not yet responded to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Earlier this week, it was reported that the Biden Administration planned to bring new export restrictions as part of efforts to prevent China and Russia from accessing advanced artificial intelligence chips from U.S. companies such as Nvidia Advanced Micro Devices (AMD).

"It makes no sense for the Biden White House to control everyday data-center computers and technology that is already in gaming PCs worldwide, disguised as an anti-China move," Finkle said.

Finkle noted that "the extreme 'country cap' policy" will affect mainstream computers in countries globally and would do nothing to promote national security but push the world to alternative technologies.

The new restrictions, which could be announced as soon as Friday, would create a tier system of which countries the U.S. can export high-end processors and the quantity they can receive, the report noted.

Earlier this week, Nvidia's CEO Jensen Huang had said that he was ready to meet Donald Trump and offer his help to the coming administration.

"I'd be delighted to go see him and congratulate him, and do whatever we can to make this administration succeed," Huang had said in an interview with Bloomberg Television. As of then, Huang hadn't been invited to visit Trump at the incoming President's home base at Mar-a-Lago in Florida, the report noted.

data.title: Biodesix estimates Q4 revenue above consensus
data.description:
• Diagnostic solutions company Biodesix (NASDAQ:BDSX) announced on Friday an estimated Q4 revenue of $20.4M, up 39% from the prior-year period.
  • The consensus revenue estimate stands at $19.8M.
  • BDSX shares were trading +1.35% pre-market at $1.50.
  • The full-year 2024 revenue is expected to be $71.3M, up 45% from 2023 and vs. $70.7M consensus.
  • The company said it had cash and cash equivalents of $26.2M as of December 31, 2024.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: ChipMOS sees 6.3% Y/Y revenue growth for 2024
data.description:
• ChipMOS TECHNOLOGIES (NASDAQ:IMOS) expects revenue growth of around 6.3% year-on-year for full-year 2024, reflecting its “continued leadership in its core markets” even as fourth quarter revenue fell.
  • Q4 revenue was $164.7M, down -5.7% Y/Y and -11% from the third quarter of 2024. The company noted that the quarterly decline reflects macro industry pricing and demand headwinds.
  • December revenue fell 3% M/M and 5.4% Y/Y to $54.3M.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Commodity Roundup: Outlook remains bearish for European gas prices, BMI says
data.description:
European natural gas prices have rallied at the start of the year, reaching a peak of EUR50/MWh on the back of increased winter demand and fears over supply following the end of the Russia-Ukraine gas transit deal.

While analysts at BMI Research acknowledge significant upside risk to prices in 2025 from below average storage levels, European markets, according to them, are well-prepared to plug the gap with increased LNG shipments, supporting the bearish outlook for TTF (NGEU:COM) and NBP prices.

Henry Hub prices in contrast have softened in the first week of January, though they remain elevated compared to 2024. BMI Research expects Henry Hub prices (NG1:COM) to increase by 41% y-o-y, from $2.4mnbtu in 2024 to $3.4mnbtu in 2025.

"The key drivers behind elevated Henry Hub front month prices are the rise in European benchmarks and cold weather in key gas-consuming U.S. states boosting domestic prices."

On the other hand, weak demand in manufacturing and other industrial sectors in Europe is seen reducing demand for natural gas, exerting additional downward pressure on prices.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (NYSEARCA:UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

Other Commodity Key Views:

While Brent crude (CO1:COM) has started 2025 on a strong footing, and is on its way to clock a fourth-straight week of gains, BMI's current forecast sees prices averaging $76.0/bbl in 2025, down from an average of $79.9/bbl in 2024.

"However, there are considerable risks to this view stemming from the uncertainties surrounding President-elect Trump’s second term in office."

Turning to industrial metals, which saw a mixed performance over the past week amid an uncertain demand outlook in Mainland China, analysts expect significant volatility in 2025 on the back of anticipated U.S. dollar strength and Trump-led trade policy shifts.

Among precious metals, BMI is bearish towards gold (XAUUSD:CUR), and expects prices to average $2,500/oz in 2025.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)
